items.title: Section 143(2) Assessment Quashed for Notice Issued by Officer Without Jurisdiction: ITAT Delhi
items.summary: ITAT Delhi quashed the assessment after holding that the Section 143(2) notice was issued by an Assessing Officer lacking jurisdiction under CBDT Instruction No. 01/2011. The post Section 143(2) Assessment Quashed for Notice Issued by Officer Without Jurisdiction: ITAT Delhi appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Legal Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, empowers the Assessing Officer (AO) to issue a notice under Section 143(2) to a taxpayer, requiring them to furnish additional information or evidence in support of their return of income. However, the AO's jurisdiction to issue such a notice is crucial, as it directly impacts the validity of the subsequent assessment proceedings. The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has issued various instructions, including Instruction No. 01/2011, to clarify the jurisdictional aspects of AOs. According to Section 120 of the Income Tax Act, the CBDT may, by notification, specify the areas or persons in respect of whom the AO shall exercise jurisdiction. Furthermore, Section 124 of the Act empowers the CBDT to transfer cases from one AO to another, ensuring that assessments are conducted by AOs with the requisite jurisdiction.

The CBDT Instruction No. 01/2011 emphasizes the importance of AOs exercising jurisdiction over taxpayers within their designated areas or having the requisite jurisdictional limits. This instruction is crucial in ensuring that assessments are conducted in a fair and transparent manner, without any jurisdictional overreach by the AOs. The Instruction also highlights the need for AOs to verify their jurisdiction before issuing notices under Section 143(2), as any lapse in this regard may render the assessment proceedings void ab initio. In the context of Section 143(2) notices, the AO's jurisdiction assumes even greater significance, as it directly affects the validity of the assessment proceedings.

It is also relevant to consider the provisions of Section 292B of the Income Tax Act, which deals with the consequences of an AO lacking jurisdiction. According to this section, if an AO lacks jurisdiction, the assessment proceedings initiated by such AO are deemed to be without jurisdiction, and the assessment order passed by such AO is liable to be quashed. This provision underscores the importance of AOs adhering to their jurisdictional limits and ensuring that they have the requisite authority to issue notices under Section 143(2). The ITAT and courts have consistently held that jurisdiction is a fundamental aspect of assessment proceedings, and any lapse in this regard may result in the assessment being set aside.

Facts of the Case

In the instant case, the AO issued a notice under Section 143(2) to the taxpayer, requiring them to furnish additional information and evidence in support of their return of income. However, the taxpayer contended that the AO lacked jurisdiction to issue the notice, as the AO was not authorized to exercise jurisdiction over the taxpayer under the CBDT Instruction No. 01/2011. The taxpayer argued that the notice was therefore invalid, and the subsequent assessment proceedings were without jurisdiction. The AO, on the other hand, contended that the notice was issued in accordance with the provisions of Section 143(2) and that the taxpayer had failed to comply with the requirements of the notice.

The taxpayer's contention was based on the premise that the AO's jurisdiction is a fundamental aspect of assessment proceedings, and any lapse in this regard may render the assessment proceedings void ab initio. The taxpayer relied on various judicial precedents, including the decisions of the Supreme Court and High Courts, which have consistently held that jurisdiction is a vital aspect of assessment proceedings. The taxpayer also highlighted the importance of the CBDT Instruction No. 01/2011, which clarifies the jurisdictional aspects of AOs and emphasizes the need for AOs to exercise jurisdiction over taxpayers within their designated areas or having the requisite jurisdictional limits.

The ITAT was required to consider the validity of the notice issued under Section 143(2) and determine whether the AO had the requisite jurisdiction to initiate assessment proceedings against the taxpayer. The ITAT had to examine the provisions of the Income Tax Act, the CBDT Instruction No. 01/2011, and relevant judicial precedents to arrive at a decision. The ITAT's decision would have significant implications for the taxpayer, as it would determine the validity of the assessment proceedings and the consequent tax liability.

Issue Before the Tribunal

The primary issue before the ITAT was whether the AO had the requisite jurisdiction to issue the notice under Section 143(2) to the taxpayer. The ITAT had to consider the provisions of the CBDT Instruction No. 01/2011 and the relevant provisions of the Income Tax Act to determine the AO's jurisdiction. The ITAT also had to examine the taxpayer's contention that the notice was invalid due to the AO's lack of jurisdiction and the consequent impact on the assessment proceedings. The ITAT's decision would depend on its interpretation of the relevant provisions and its analysis of the facts and circumstances of the case.

The ITAT had to consider the principles of natural justice and the requirements of a valid assessment proceeding. The ITAT had to determine whether the AO's lack of jurisdiction was a fatal flaw that rendered the assessment proceedings void ab initio. The ITAT also had to consider the taxpayer's rights and obligations under the Income Tax Act and the consequences of the AO's actions on the taxpayer's tax liability. The ITAT's decision would provide clarity on the importance of jurisdiction in assessment proceedings and the need for AOs to adhere to their jurisdictional limits.

The issue before the ITAT was not only significant for the taxpayer but also had broader implications for the tax administration and the assessment proceedings. The ITAT's decision would provide guidance on the importance of jurisdiction and the need for AOs to exercise caution when issuing notices under Section 143(2). The decision would also underscore the importance of taxpayers verifying the AO's jurisdiction before responding to notices and participating in assessment proceedings. The ITAT's ruling would have a significant impact on the tax landscape and would be closely watched by taxpayers, AOs, and tax practitioners.

Arguments & Contentions

The taxpayer argued that the AO lacked jurisdiction to issue the notice under Section 143(2), as the AO was not authorized to exercise jurisdiction over the taxpayer under the CBDT Instruction No. 01/2011. The taxpayer contended that the notice was therefore invalid, and the subsequent assessment proceedings were without jurisdiction. The taxpayer relied on various judicial precedents, including the decisions of the Supreme Court and High Courts, which have consistently held that jurisdiction is a vital aspect of assessment proceedings. The taxpayer also highlighted the importance of the CBDT Instruction No. 01/2011, which clarifies the jurisdictional aspects of AOs and emphasizes the need for AOs to exercise jurisdiction over taxpayers within their designated areas or having the requisite jurisdictional limits.

The AO, on the other hand, contended that the notice was issued in accordance with the provisions of Section 143(2) and that the taxpayer had failed to comply with the requirements of the notice. The AO argued that the taxpayer's contention regarding the AO's lack of jurisdiction was not tenable, as the AO had the requisite authority to issue the notice under Section 143(2). The AO also relied on various judicial precedents, which have held that the AO's jurisdiction is not limited by the CBDT Instruction No. 01/2011, and that the AO has the discretion to issue notices under Section 143(2) to any taxpayer, regardless of their jurisdictional limits.

The ITAT had to carefully consider the arguments and contentions of both parties and examine the relevant provisions of the Income Tax Act, the CBDT Instruction No. 01/2011, and judicial precedents to arrive at a decision. The ITAT's decision would depend on its interpretation of the relevant provisions and its analysis of the facts and circumstances of the case. The ITAT's ruling would provide clarity on the importance of jurisdiction in assessment proceedings and the need for AOs to adhere to their jurisdictional limits.

Tribunal's Ruling & Reasoning

The ITAT ruled in favor of the taxpayer, holding that the AO lacked jurisdiction to issue the notice under Section 143(2). The ITAT relied on the CBDT Instruction No. 01/2011, which clarifies the jurisdictional aspects of AOs and emphasizes the need for AOs to exercise jurisdiction over taxpayers within their designated areas or having the requisite jurisdictional limits. The ITAT held that the AO's lack of jurisdiction was a fatal flaw that rendered the assessment proceedings void ab initio. The ITAT also relied on various judicial precedents, which have consistently held that jurisdiction is a vital aspect of assessment proceedings.

The ITAT's ruling was based on a careful analysis of the relevant provisions of the Income Tax Act, the CBDT Instruction No. 01/2011, and judicial precedents. The ITAT considered the principles of natural justice and the requirements of a valid assessment proceeding. The ITAT held that the AO's lack of jurisdiction was a fundamental flaw that could not be cured by any subsequent actions or proceedings. The ITAT's decision was a significant victory for the taxpayer, as it resulted in the quashing of the assessment proceedings and the consequent tax liability.

The ITAT's ruling has significant implications for tax administration and assessment proceedings. The ruling underscores the importance of AOs adhering to their jurisdictional limits and exercising caution when issuing notices under Section 143(2). The ruling also highlights the need for taxpayers to verify the AO's jurisdiction before responding to notices and participating in assessment proceedings. The ITAT's decision provides clarity on the importance of jurisdiction in assessment proceedings and will have a significant impact on the tax landscape.

items.title: Can Section 148A Reassessment Be Based Solely on Red-Flagged Information?
items.summary: Ahmedabad ITAT quashed reassessment where the Assessing Officer relied on red-flagged information without independent inquiry under Section 148A. The post Can Section 148A Reassessment Be Based Solely on Red-Flagged Information? appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, has undergone significant amendments in recent years, particularly with the introduction of Section 148A, which deals with the reassessment of income. This provision was inserted to provide a framework for the Assessing Officer to reopen assessments based on certain criteria. Section 148A(a) empowers the Assessing Officer to issue a notice for reassessment if they have "reason to believe" that income has escaped assessment. This reason to believe must be based on tangible material or information that suggests the escapement of income.

Section 147 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, which governs the reassessment of income, has been subject to various interpretations by courts and tribunals. The Supreme Court, in the case of Raymonds Ltd. vs. Commissioner of Income Tax, has held that the Assessing Officer must have "reason to believe" that income has escaped assessment, and this reason must be based on tangible material. The introduction of Section 148A has further clarified the procedure for reassessment, emphasizing the need for the Assessing Officer to conduct an independent inquiry before issuing a notice under Section 148.

The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has also issued circulars and guidelines to clarify the procedure for reassessment under Section 148A. For instance, the CBDT has emphasized the importance of conducting an independent inquiry by the Assessing Officer before issuing a notice under Section 148. This inquiry must be based on tangible material or information that suggests the escapement of income. The CBDT has also cautioned against mechanical reliance on red-flagged information without verifying its accuracy or relevance.

The Development in Detail

The Ahmedabad ITAT's decision to quash a reassessment notice issued under Section 148A is significant, as it highlights the importance of independent inquiry by the Assessing Officer. The tribunal held that the Assessing Officer had relied solely on red-flagged information without conducting any independent inquiry, which is a prerequisite under Section 148A. The ITAT observed that the Assessing Officer had not applied their mind to the information available and had mechanically issued the notice, which is not in accordance with the provisions of Section 148A.

The ITAT's decision is based on the principle that the Assessing Officer must have "reason to believe" that income has escaped assessment, and this reason must be based on tangible material or information. The tribunal held that the Assessing Officer's reliance on red-flagged information without verifying its accuracy or relevance does not constitute a valid reason to believe. This decision is consistent with the Supreme Court's judgment in Raymonds Ltd. vs. Commissioner of Income Tax, which emphasizes the importance of tangible material in forming a reason to believe.

The Ahmedabad ITAT's decision has significant implications for taxpayers and Assessing Officers. It underscores the need for the Assessing Officer to conduct an independent inquiry before issuing a notice under Section 148A. This inquiry must be based on tangible material or information that suggests the escapement of income, and not merely on red-flagged information. The decision also highlights the importance of verifying the accuracy and relevance of information before relying on it as a basis for reassessment.

Key Provisions & Impact

Section 148A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, is a critical provision that deals with the reassessment of income. The provision empowers the Assessing Officer to issue a notice for reassessment if they have "reason to believe" that income has escaped assessment. The reason to believe must be based on tangible material or information that suggests the escapement of income. The Assessing Officer must conduct an independent inquiry before issuing a notice under Section 148A, and this inquiry must be based on tangible material or information.

The Ahmedabad ITAT's decision has significant implications for taxpayers and Assessing Officers. It highlights the need for the Assessing Officer to conduct an independent inquiry before issuing a notice under Section 148A. The decision also emphasizes the importance of verifying the accuracy and relevance of information before relying on it as a basis for reassessment. The CBDT's guidelines and circulars also emphasize the importance of independent inquiry and verification of information.

The impact of the Ahmedabad ITAT's decision is far-reaching. It underscores the need for the Assessing Officer to apply their mind to the information available and not mechanically rely on red-flagged information. The decision also highlights the importance of transparency and accountability in the reassessment process. Taxpayers can now challenge reassessment notices that are issued without proper inquiry or verification of information. The decision also cautions Assessing Officers against mechanical reliance on red-flagged information and emphasizes the need for independent inquiry and verification of information.

Critical Analysis

The Ahmedabad ITAT's decision is a significant development in the context of reassessment under Section 148A. The decision highlights the importance of independent inquiry and verification of information before issuing a notice under Section 148A. The ITAT's decision is consistent with the Supreme Court's judgment in Raymonds Ltd. vs. Commissioner of Income Tax, which emphasizes the importance of tangible material in forming a reason to believe.

The decision also raises important questions about the role of technology in the reassessment process. The use of red-flagged information and automated systems can lead to mechanical reliance on information without proper verification. The ITAT's decision cautions against such an approach and emphasizes the need for human intervention and independent inquiry in the reassessment process.

The decision also has implications for the CBDT's guidelines and circulars on reassessment under Section 148A. The CBDT may need to revisit its guidelines and circulars to ensure that they are consistent with the ITAT's decision. The CBDT may also need to provide further clarification on the procedure for independent inquiry and verification of information in the reassessment process.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Ahmedabad ITAT's decision has significant compliance implications for taxpayers and Assessing Officers. Taxpayers must ensure that they maintain accurate and complete records to support their tax returns. Taxpayers must also be prepared to respond to reassessment notices and challenge them if they are issued without proper inquiry or verification of information.

Assessing Officers must ensure that they conduct an independent inquiry before issuing a notice under Section 148A. Assessing Officers must also verify the accuracy and relevance of information before relying on it as a basis for reassessment. The following are key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Ensure that clients maintain accurate and complete records to support their tax returns.

2. Verify the accuracy and relevance of information before relying on it as a basis for reassessment.

3. Conduct an independent inquiry before issuing a notice under Section 148A.

4. Challenge reassessment notices that are issued without proper inquiry or verification of information.

5. Ensure that clients are prepared to respond to reassessment notices and challenge them if necessary.

items.title: No Capital Gains Without Transfer Under Development Agreement: ITAT Pune
items.summary: ITAT Pune deleted capital gains holding no transfer occurred under Sections 2(47)(v) or 2(47)(vi) as no possession or consideration was received. The post No Capital Gains Without Transfer Under Development Agreement: ITAT Pune appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Legal Framework

The concept of capital gains tax is intricately linked with the notion of "transfer" as defined under the Income Tax Act, 1961. Section 2(47) of the Act exhaustively lists the various modes through which a transfer can take place, including sale, exchange, relinquishment, and any other manner of transfer. Specifically, Sections 2(47)(v) and 2(47)(vi) deal with the transfer of capital assets through development agreements, which have become a common practice in real estate transactions. These provisions are crucial in determining whether a capital gains tax liability arises in such scenarios. The CBDT has also issued circulars and guidelines to clarify the applicability of these provisions, emphasizing the importance of understanding the legal nuances involved.

Furthermore, the Supreme Court has, in several landmark judgments, interpreted the scope of "transfer" under the Income Tax Act, providing valuable insights for tax practitioners. For instance, the Court has held that the term "transfer" encompasses not just a conveyance but also includes the transfer of rights in a capital asset. This expansive interpretation underscores the need for a meticulous analysis of the facts and circumstances of each case to ascertain whether a transfer has indeed occurred, thereby triggering capital gains tax liability. The implications of these judicial pronouncements are far-reaching, influencing not just taxation but also the structuring of business transactions.

In the context of development agreements, the question of whether a transfer occurs is often contentious. The Income Tax Act does not provide a specific definition of a development agreement, leaving it to judicial interpretation and the application of general principles. The ITAT and courts have, through their decisions, attempted to clarify the position, though the absence of clear-cut guidelines means that each case is decided on its merits. This ambiguity necessitates a deep understanding of the legal framework, including relevant provisions such as Section 45 of the Income Tax Act, which deals with capital gains, and the concept of "possession" as a critical factor in determining the occurrence of a transfer.

Facts of the Case

The ITAT Pune judgment revolves around a dispute concerning the tax implications of a development agreement. The taxpayer had entered into an agreement with a developer for the development of their property. The agreement stipulated that the developer would construct a building on the property and, in consideration, would receive a certain number of flats. The taxpayer did not receive any monetary consideration or possession of the property. The Assessing Officer (AO) took the view that this transaction amounted to a transfer of the capital asset, attracting capital gains tax under the provisions of the Income Tax Act.

The taxpayer contested this assessment, arguing that no transfer had occurred as there was no change in possession or receipt of consideration. The taxpayer relied on the provisions of Sections 2(47)(v) and 2(47)(vi) to support their claim, emphasizing that these sections specifically deal with the transfer of assets through development agreements and require the actual receipt of consideration or possession for a transfer to be considered as having taken place. The taxpayer also cited relevant case law to bolster their argument, highlighting the importance of interpreting the provisions of the Act in harmony with judicial precedents.

The facts of the case underscore the complexities involved in determining whether a transfer has occurred in the context of development agreements. The absence of monetary consideration or change in possession raises critical questions about the applicability of capital gains tax provisions. The taxpayer's arguments and the AO's stance reflect the divergent views on the interpretation of the Act's provisions, necessitating a detailed examination of the legal framework and relevant case law to resolve the dispute.

Issue Before the Tribunal

The primary issue before the ITAT Pune was to determine whether the development agreement between the taxpayer and the developer constituted a "transfer" within the meaning of Section 2(47) of the Income Tax Act, 1961. The tribunal had to consider whether the absence of possession or consideration received by the taxpayer was sufficient to negate the occurrence of a transfer. This required an in-depth analysis of the provisions of the Act, particularly Sections 2(47)(v) and 2(47)(vi), and an understanding of the judicial interpretations of these sections.

The tribunal also had to evaluate the taxpayer's contention that the development agreement did not result in any capital gains, as there was no transfer of the capital asset. This involved examining the terms of the agreement, the intentions of the parties, and the actual outcome of the transaction. The ITAT's decision would have significant implications for the taxpayer and could potentially impact similar transactions, highlighting the need for clarity and consistency in the application of tax laws.

The issue at hand was not merely a matter of interpreting the Income Tax Act but also involved considering the broader implications of the decision on the taxpayer and the tax authorities. The tribunal's ruling would provide valuable guidance on the tax treatment of development agreements, an area where clarity is essential for both taxpayers and tax administrators. The decision would also reflect the tribunal's approach to interpreting the Act's provisions in light of judicial precedents and the principles of taxation.

Arguments & Contentions

The taxpayer argued that the development agreement did not constitute a transfer as defined under the Income Tax Act. They contended that the absence of possession or consideration was crucial in determining whether a transfer had occurred. The taxpayer relied on the provisions of Sections 2(47)(v) and 2(47)(vi), which, according to them, necessitate the actual receipt of consideration or possession for a transfer to be deemed as having taken place. The taxpayer also submitted that the AO's assessment was erroneous, as it failed to consider the specific terms of the development agreement and the legal implications thereof.

The revenue authorities, on the other hand, contended that the development agreement resulted in a transfer of the capital asset, attracting capital gains tax liability. They argued that the taxpayer's interpretation of the provisions was narrow and did not align with the intent of the legislation. The revenue authorities also cited case law to support their stance, emphasizing that the courts have adopted a broader interpretation of the term "transfer" to include transactions that may not involve direct consideration or possession.

The arguments presented by both sides highlighted the complexities and nuances involved in interpreting the provisions of the Income Tax Act. The taxpayer's contentions underscored the importance of a literal interpretation of the Act's provisions, while the revenue authorities' arguments reflected a more expansive view of the term "transfer". The tribunal's decision would ultimately depend on its interpretation of the Act's provisions, the application of judicial precedents, and the specific facts of the case.

Tribunal's Ruling & Reasoning

The ITAT Pune ruled in favor of the taxpayer, holding that no capital gains arose from the development agreement. The tribunal found that the agreement did not result in a transfer of the capital asset, as there was no change in possession or receipt of consideration by the taxpayer. The tribunal's decision was based on a detailed analysis of the provisions of Sections 2(47)(v) and 2(47)(vi) of the Income Tax Act, which, according to the tribunal, clearly require the actual receipt of consideration or possession for a transfer to be considered as having taken place.

The tribunal also considered the judicial precedents cited by both parties and observed that the courts have indeed adopted a more expansive interpretation of the term "transfer". However, the tribunal distinguished the present case on the grounds that the specific facts and circumstances did not warrant the application of such an interpretation. The tribunal's reasoning was centered on the principle that the provisions of the Act must be interpreted in a manner that gives effect to the legislative intent, while also ensuring that the taxpayer is not unfairly burdened with tax liabilities.

The tribunal's ruling and reasoning provided clarity on the tax implications of development agreements, emphasizing the importance of considering the specific facts and circumstances of each case. The decision underscored the need for a nuanced approach to interpreting the provisions of the Income Tax Act, one that balances the legislative intent with the need to ensure fairness and equity in taxation. The ruling would have significant implications for taxpayers and tax practitioners, offering guidance on how to structure development agreements to minimize tax liabilities while ensuring compliance with the Act's provisions.

Key Takeaways for Practitioners

The ITAT Pune judgment offers several key takeaways for practitioners. Firstly, it is essential to carefully examine the terms of development agreements to determine whether they result in a transfer of the capital asset. Secondly, the absence of possession or consideration received by the taxpayer can be a critical factor in negating the occurrence of a transfer. Thirdly, practitioners must be aware of the judicial precedents and the provisions of the Income Tax Act, particularly Sections 2(47)(v) and 2(47)(vi), when advising clients on the tax implications of development agreements.

Here are some concrete action points for practitioners:

1. **Review development agreements**: Carefully review the terms of development agreements to identify potential tax implications and ensure compliance with the Income Tax Act.

2. **Consider the receipt of consideration**: Determine whether the taxpayer has received any consideration, including monetary payments or possession, to assess whether a transfer has occurred.

3. **Apply the provisions of Sections 2(47)(v) and 2(47)(vi)**: Ensure that the provisions of these sections are applied correctly to determine whether a transfer has taken place, considering the specific facts and circumstances of the case.

items.title: EPF, EPS & EDLI Framework Under Code on Social Security, 2020
items.summary: Government notifies EPF, EPS and EDLI Schemes, 2026 under the Code on Social Security, 2020 with digital compliance and preserved existing benefits. The post EPF, EPS & EDLI Framework Under Code on Social Security, 2020 appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Code on Social Security, 2020, is a landmark legislation that aims to consolidate and amend the laws relating to social security, with a view to providing universal social security coverage to all workers, including those in the unorganized sector. The Code repeals and replaces nine existing laws, including the Employees' Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act, 1952, which governed the Employees' Provident Fund (EPF), Employees' Pension Scheme (EPS), and Employees' Deposit Linked Insurance Scheme (EDLI). The EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes have been notified under the Code on Social Security, 2020, with effect from 2026, marking a significant development in the social security framework for employees in India.

Section 142 of the Code on Social Security, 2020, empowers the Central Government to frame schemes for providing social security benefits to employees, including provident fund, pension, and insurance benefits. The EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes notified under the Code are intended to provide social security benefits to employees in the organized sector, with the EPF scheme providing a retirement savings benefit, the EPS scheme providing a pension benefit, and the EDLI scheme providing an insurance benefit in the event of death. The schemes are administered by the Employees' Provident Fund Organization (EPFO), a statutory body established under the Employees' Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act, 1952.

The notification of the EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes under the Code on Social Security, 2020, is a significant development, as it seeks to preserve the existing benefits of the schemes while introducing digital compliance measures to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of the schemes. The move is expected to benefit millions of employees in the organized sector, who are covered under the schemes, and is in line with the government's objective of providing universal social security coverage to all workers.

The Development in Detail

The EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes notified under the Code on Social Security, 2020, retain the core features of the existing schemes, including the contribution rates, benefit structures, and eligibility criteria. The EPF scheme requires employees to contribute 12% of their basic wages and dearness allowance to the fund, with a matching contribution from the employer. The EPS scheme provides a pension benefit to employees who have completed 10 years of service and have attained the age of 58 years, with the pension amount calculated based on the employee's salary and service period.

The EDLI scheme provides an insurance benefit to employees in the event of death, with the benefit amount calculated based on the employee's salary and service period. The schemes are funded by the contributions made by employees and employers, as well as the interest earned on the investments made by the EPFO. The EPFO is responsible for administering the schemes, including collecting contributions, investing the funds, and paying out benefits to eligible employees.

The notification of the EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes under the Code on Social Security, 2020, introduces digital compliance measures, including online registration, contribution payment, and benefit claim processing. The move is expected to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of the schemes, reduce administrative costs, and enhance the overall experience of employees and employers. The digital compliance measures are also expected to facilitate greater transparency and accountability in the administration of the schemes.

Key Provisions & Impact

The EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes notified under the Code on Social Security, 2020, have significant implications for employees and employers in the organized sector. The schemes provide a social security safety net for employees, helping them to save for retirement, receive a pension benefit, and access insurance coverage in the event of death. The schemes also impose obligations on employers, including the requirement to contribute to the funds and comply with the administrative requirements of the schemes.

Section 465 of the Code on Social Security, 2020, provides that the EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes shall be governed by the provisions of the Code, and that the Central Government may frame rules and regulations to administer the schemes. The section also empowers the Central Government to appoint an authority to administer the schemes and to resolve disputes arising under the schemes. The provisions of the Code and the rules and regulations framed thereunder shall override any inconsistent provisions of the existing laws and schemes.

The notification of the EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes under the Code on Social Security, 2020, is expected to have a positive impact on the social security landscape in India, providing greater coverage and benefits to employees in the organized sector. The move is also expected to promote greater compliance and adherence to the schemes, reducing the risk of disputes and litigation. However, the implementation of the schemes and the digital compliance measures will require careful planning and execution to ensure a smooth transition and to minimize disruptions to employees and employers.

Critical Analysis

The notification of the EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes under the Code on Social Security, 2020, marks a significant development in the social security framework for employees in India. The move is expected to provide greater coverage and benefits to employees in the organized sector, while also promoting greater compliance and adherence to the schemes. However, the implementation of the schemes and the digital compliance measures will require careful planning and execution to ensure a smooth transition and to minimize disruptions to employees and employers.

Section 2(24) of the Income Tax Act, 1961, defines "income" to include any income from a provident fund, pension fund, or other retirement benefit scheme. The EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes notified under the Code on Social Security, 2020, are subject to the provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961, and the benefits paid out under the schemes may be taxable in the hands of the employees. The tax implications of the schemes will need to be carefully considered by employees and employers to ensure compliance with the tax laws and to minimize tax liabilities.

The notification of the EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes under the Code on Social Security, 2020, also raises important questions about the role of the EPFO in administering the schemes and the accountability of the organization to employees and employers. The EPFO is a statutory body established under the Employees' Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act, 1952, and is responsible for administering the EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes. The organization is also responsible for investing the funds contributed to the schemes and for paying out benefits to eligible employees.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The notification of the EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes under the Code on Social Security, 2020, has significant compliance implications for employees and employers in the organized sector. Employers will need to ensure that they are complying with the administrative requirements of the schemes, including online registration, contribution payment, and benefit claim processing. Employees will need to ensure that they are eligible for the benefits under the schemes and that they are receiving the correct benefits.

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Review the EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes notified under the Code on Social Security, 2020, to understand the core features, contribution rates, benefit structures, and eligibility criteria.

2. Ensure compliance with the administrative requirements of the schemes, including online registration, contribution payment, and benefit claim processing.

3. Consider the tax implications of the schemes and ensure compliance with the tax laws to minimize tax liabilities.

4. Review the role of the EPFO in administering the schemes and the accountability of the organization to employees and employers.

5. Provide advisory services to employees and employers on the EPF, EPS, and EDLI schemes, including eligibility, benefits, and compliance requirements.

items.title: Charitable Trust Exemptions under Income-tax Act, 2025 (TY 2026-27)
items.summary: Explains the RNPO exemption framework under the Income-tax Act, 2025, covering Application, Deemed Application, Accumulation and tax computation. The post Charitable Trust Exemptions under Income-tax Act, 2025 (TY 2026-27) appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Income-tax Act, 2025, has introduced significant changes to the exemptions available to charitable trusts, with a focus on streamlining the process and ensuring that genuine charitable activities are not unduly burdened. Section 10(23C) of the Income-tax Act, 1961, which has been carried forward to the 2025 Act, provides exemptions to charitable trusts, subject to certain conditions. The newly introduced concept of Registered Non-Profit Organisations (RNPOs) under the Income-tax Act, 2025, is expected to play a crucial role in the exemption framework. To understand the implications of these changes, it is essential to delve into the statutory framework and the relevant provisions of the Act.

The RNPO exemption framework is built upon the foundation laid by Section 11 of the Income-tax Act, 1961, which deals with the income of charitable trusts. This section provides that the income of a charitable trust, which is applied for charitable purposes, shall be exempt from tax. However, the exemption is subject to certain conditions, including the requirement that the trust must be registered under Section 12A of the Act. The introduction of the RNPO concept under the Income-tax Act, 2025, is expected to simplify the registration process and provide a more streamlined approach to exemptions.

The CBDT has also issued circulars and guidelines to clarify the application of the exemption provisions, including the procedure for registration and the conditions for availing exemptions. For instance, Circular No. 7/2010, dated October 27, 2010, provides guidance on the registration of charitable trusts under Section 12A of the Act. These circulars and guidelines are essential in understanding the practical implications of the exemption framework and the compliance requirements for charitable trusts.

The Development in Detail

The Income-tax Act, 2025, introduces the concept of Deemed Application, which allows charitable trusts to claim exemptions without having to explicitly apply for them. This provision is expected to simplify the process and reduce the compliance burden on charitable trusts. However, the Deemed Application provision is subject to certain conditions, including the requirement that the trust must have been registered under the Act for at least three years. The introduction of this provision is a significant development, as it recognizes the importance of charitable activities and seeks to facilitate their growth.

The Accumulation provision under Section 11(2) of the Income-tax Act, 1961, has also been carried forward to the 2025 Act. This provision allows charitable trusts to accumulate up to 15% of their income for future application, subject to certain conditions. The Accumulation provision is essential, as it enables charitable trusts to plan for future activities and ensure the sustainability of their operations. The CBDT has also issued guidelines on the application of the Accumulation provision, including the procedure for calculating the accumulated income and the conditions for its utilization.

The tax computation provisions under the Income-tax Act, 2025, have also been modified to take into account the exemptions available to charitable trusts. Section 145A of the Act provides that the income of a charitable trust, which is exempt under Section 10(23C), shall not be included in the total income for tax computation purposes. This provision is crucial, as it ensures that charitable trusts are not subject to tax on their exempt income. The CBDT has also issued circulars and guidelines to clarify the tax computation provisions, including the procedure for calculating the taxable income of charitable trusts.

Key Provisions & Impact

The RNPO exemption framework under the Income-tax Act, 2025, is expected to have a significant impact on charitable trusts and the non-profit sector as a whole. The introduction of the Deemed Application provision and the Accumulation provision is expected to simplify the process and reduce the compliance burden on charitable trusts. However, the conditions attached to these provisions, including the requirement for registration and the accumulation limits, must be carefully considered to ensure that charitable trusts can avail of the exemptions.

The tax computation provisions under the Act are also critical, as they determine the taxable income of charitable trusts. Section 145A of the Act provides that the income of a charitable trust, which is exempt under Section 10(23C), shall not be included in the total income for tax computation purposes. This provision is essential, as it ensures that charitable trusts are not subject to tax on their exempt income. The CBDT has also issued guidelines on the tax computation provisions, including the procedure for calculating the taxable income of charitable trusts.

The implications of the RNPO exemption framework extend beyond the charitable trusts themselves, as they also impact the donors and the beneficiaries of charitable activities. The introduction of the RNPO concept is expected to increase transparency and accountability in the non-profit sector, which is essential for building trust and ensuring the effective utilization of resources. The CBDT has also issued circulars and guidelines to clarify the implications of the RNPO exemption framework, including the procedure for registration and the conditions for availing exemptions.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the RNPO exemption framework under the Income-tax Act, 2025, reveals that the provisions are designed to facilitate the growth of charitable activities while ensuring transparency and accountability. The introduction of the Deemed Application provision and the Accumulation provision is expected to simplify the process and reduce the compliance burden on charitable trusts. However, the conditions attached to these provisions, including the requirement for registration and the accumulation limits, must be carefully considered to ensure that charitable trusts can avail of the exemptions.

The tax computation provisions under the Act are also subject to critical analysis, as they determine the taxable income of charitable trusts. Section 145A of the Act provides that the income of a charitable trust, which is exempt under Section 10(23C), shall not be included in the total income for tax computation purposes. This provision is essential, as it ensures that charitable trusts are not subject to tax on their exempt income. However, the CBDT must issue clear guidelines on the tax computation provisions to avoid any ambiguity or confusion.

The RNPO exemption framework also raises important questions about the role of the state in regulating charitable activities. The introduction of the RNPO concept is expected to increase transparency and accountability in the non-profit sector, which is essential for building trust and ensuring the effective utilization of resources. However, the state must also ensure that the regulatory framework is not overly burdensome, as this could stifle the growth of charitable activities and undermine their effectiveness.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The RNPO exemption framework under the Income-tax Act, 2025, has significant compliance implications for charitable trusts and the non-profit sector as a whole. To ensure compliance with the provisions, charitable trusts must register under the Act and meet the conditions for availing exemptions. The following are key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Registration: Charitable trusts must register under the Income-tax Act, 2025, to avail of the exemptions. The registration process involves filing an application with the CBDT, which must be accompanied by the required documents and information.

2. Deemed Application: Charitable trusts can claim exemptions under the Deemed Application provision, subject to certain conditions. Practitioners must carefully consider these conditions to ensure that their clients can avail of the exemptions.

3. Accumulation: Charitable trusts can accumulate up to 15% of their income for future application, subject to certain conditions. Practitioners must ensure that their clients comply with these conditions to avoid any tax implications.

4. Tax Computation: The tax computation provisions under the Act must be carefully considered to ensure that charitable trusts are not subject to tax on their exempt income. Practitioners must ensure that their clients comply with the tax computation provisions to avoid any tax implications.

5. Documentation: Charitable trusts must maintain proper documentation to support their claims for exemptions. Practitioners must ensure that their clients maintain accurate and complete records to avoid any tax implications.

items.title: BPO Services to Overseas Client Are Exports, Not Intermediary Services: CESTAT Chandigarh
items.summary: CESTAT Chandigarh set aside denial of ₹14.55 crore refund, holding BPO services were not intermediary services and qualified as export. The post BPO Services to Overseas Client Are Exports, Not Intermediary Services: CESTAT Chandigarh appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The distinction between intermediary services and export of services has been a contentious issue under the GST regime, with significant implications for businesses providing services to overseas clients. Section 2(6) of the IGST Act defines "export of services" as the supply of services when the supplier of service is located in India, the recipient of service is located outside India, the place of supply of service is outside India, the payment for such service has been received by the supplier of service in convertible foreign exchange or in Indian rupees wherever permitted by the Reserve Bank of India, and the supplier of service and the recipient of service are not merely establishments of a distinct person in accordance with Section 8 of the IGST Act. On the other hand, Section 2(13) of the IGST Act defines "intermediary" as a broker, an agent, or any other person, by whatever name called, who arranges or facilitates the supply of goods or services or both, or securities, between two or more persons.

The tax treatment of Business Process Outsourcing (BPO) services has been a subject of debate, with the authorities often taking a view that such services are in the nature of intermediary services, thereby attracting a higher tax liability. However, the recent decision of the CESTAT Chandigarh has brought much-needed clarity on this issue. The tribunal held that BPO services provided to an overseas client qualify as export of services, and not as intermediary services, thereby allowing the supplier to claim a refund of the tax paid.

This decision is significant, as it has implications for the tax liability of BPO service providers and the availability of input tax credit. Section 16 of the IGST Act allows for the availment of input tax credit on goods and services used in the course or furtherance of business, subject to certain conditions. However, the availment of input tax credit is not available on intermediary services, as they are not considered to be in the course or furtherance of business. The CESTAT decision, therefore, has a direct impact on the tax liability and the availability of input tax credit for BPO service providers.

The Development in Detail

The CESTAT Chandigarh decision was in the context of a refund claim of ₹14.55 crore filed by a BPO service provider. The supplier had provided BPO services to an overseas client and had paid tax on the same. However, the tax authorities had denied the refund claim on the ground that the services provided were in the nature of intermediary services, and not export of services. The supplier had contested this decision before the CESTAT, which has now allowed the refund claim.

The tribunal held that the services provided by the supplier were not in the nature of intermediary services, as the supplier was not merely arranging or facilitating the supply of services between two or more persons. The supplier had provided BPO services, which included data entry, data processing, and other back-office services, which were export of services in terms of Section 2(6) of the IGST Act. The tribunal also observed that the payment for the services had been received by the supplier in convertible foreign exchange, which is a necessary condition for a supply to be considered as an export of services.

The decision of the CESTAT Chandigarh is a significant development, as it provides clarity on the tax treatment of BPO services provided to overseas clients. The decision is based on a detailed analysis of the statutory provisions and the facts of the case, and it has implications for the tax liability and the availability of input tax credit for BPO service providers. The decision is also significant, as it highlights the importance of proper documentation and record-keeping in support of a refund claim.

Key Provisions & Impact

The CESTAT decision is based on a detailed analysis of the statutory provisions, including Section 2(6) of the IGST Act, which defines "export of services", and Section 2(13) of the IGST Act, which defines "intermediary". The decision also relies on the provisions of Section 16 of the IGST Act, which allows for the availment of input tax credit on goods and services used in the course or furtherance of business. The decision has significant implications for the tax liability and the availability of input tax credit for BPO service providers.

The decision is also significant, as it highlights the importance of proper documentation and record-keeping in support of a refund claim. The supplier had maintained detailed records of the services provided, including the agreement with the overseas client, the invoices raised, and the payment received. The supplier had also filed a detailed refund claim, which included all the necessary documents and information. The CESTAT decision, therefore, emphasizes the importance of maintaining proper records and filing a detailed refund claim.

The impact of the CESTAT decision is not limited to BPO service providers, but it has implications for all service providers who provide services to overseas clients. The decision provides clarity on the tax treatment of export of services and highlights the importance of proper documentation and record-keeping in support of a refund claim. The decision is also significant, as it has implications for the tax authorities, who must now re-examine their approach to refund claims filed by service providers.

Critical Analysis

The CESTAT decision is a significant development, as it provides clarity on the tax treatment of BPO services provided to overseas clients. The decision is based on a detailed analysis of the statutory provisions and the facts of the case, and it has implications for the tax liability and the availability of input tax credit for BPO service providers. However, the decision also raises certain questions, including the definition of "intermediary services" and the tax treatment of services provided to overseas clients.

The decision of the CESTAT Chandigarh is also significant, as it highlights the importance of proper documentation and record-keeping in support of a refund claim. The supplier had maintained detailed records of the services provided, including the agreement with the overseas client, the invoices raised, and the payment received. The supplier had also filed a detailed refund claim, which included all the necessary documents and information. The CESTAT decision, therefore, emphasizes the importance of maintaining proper records and filing a detailed refund claim.

The CESTAT decision is also significant, as it has implications for the tax authorities, who must now re-examine their approach to refund claims filed by service providers. The tax authorities must ensure that they have a clear understanding of the statutory provisions and the facts of the case, before denying a refund claim. The tax authorities must also ensure that they provide a detailed reasoning for denying a refund claim, which includes all the necessary documents and information. The CESTAT decision, therefore, emphasizes the importance of transparency and accountability in the tax administration.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The CESTAT decision has significant implications for service providers who provide services to overseas clients. The decision provides clarity on the tax treatment of export of services and highlights the importance of proper documentation and record-keeping in support of a refund claim. The following are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Service providers must ensure that they maintain detailed records of the services provided, including the agreement with the overseas client, the invoices raised, and the payment received.

2. Service providers must file a detailed refund claim, which includes all the necessary documents and information, including the agreement with the overseas client, the invoices raised, and the payment received.

3. Service providers must ensure that they have a clear understanding of the statutory provisions, including Section 2(6) of the IGST Act, which defines "export of services", and Section 2(13) of the IGST Act, which defines "intermediary".

4. Service providers must ensure that they comply with all the necessary conditions for a supply to be considered as an export of services, including the payment for the services being received in convertible foreign exchange.

5. Service providers must ensure that they are aware of the tax treatment of export of services and the availability of input tax credit, and they must plan their tax strategy accordingly.

items.title: NCLT Mumbai Approves Demerger of Sarda Dairy into Chhatisgarh Investments
items.summary: NCLT Mumbai sanctioned a composite scheme under Sections 230–232 and 66 after finding statutory compliance and no objections from stakeholders. The post NCLT Mumbai Approves Demerger of Sarda Dairy into Chhatisgarh Investments appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The recent approval of the demerger of Sarda Dairy into Chhatisgarh Investments by the NCLT Mumbai has brought to the forefront the complexities and nuances of corporate restructuring under the Income Tax Act, 1961. Section 2(19AA) of the Act defines a demerger, and Section 72A provides for the carry forward and set off of losses in the case of a demerger. Furthermore, the provisions of Section 230-232 of the Companies Act, 2013, govern the process of mergers and demergers, including the requirement for approval by the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT). The NCLT's sanction of the composite scheme is a testament to the robust framework in place for facilitating corporate restructuring while ensuring compliance with statutory requirements.

The Income Tax Act, 1961, also provides for tax neutrality in cases of demergers, as outlined in Section 47(vi), which states that a demerger shall not be regarded as a transfer. items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, provides a framework for the assessment of income tax in India. Section 143(2) of the Act is a critical provision that mandates the issuance of a notice to the taxpayer if the Assessing Officer intends to make an assessment under Section 143(3). This notice is a prerequisite for a regular assessment, allowing the taxpayer to respond to the proposed adjustments. In contrast, Section 158BC of the Act deals with the assessment of income that is deemed to be income from an undisclosed foreign bank account. This provision is part of the block assessment scheme, which enables the Assessing Officer to assess income that has escaped assessment in the past.

The Patna High Court's recent judgment highlights the importance of issuing a Section 143(2) notice in the context of a Section 158BC assessment. The court's decision is significant, as it clarifies the interplay between these two provisions. To understand the implications of this judgment, it is essential to examine the statutory framework and relevant case law. Section 292BB of the Act provides that a defect or omission in a notice or order can be cured if the taxpayer has not been prejudiced. However, the court's decision suggests that the failure to issue a Section 143(2) notice is a fundamental flaw that cannot be cured under Section 292BB.

The distinction between a regular assessment under Section 143(3) and a block assessment under Section 158BC is crucial. While a regular assessment is governed by the provisions of Section 143, a block assessment is a summary procedure that enables the Assessing Officer to assess income that has escaped assessment. The block assessment scheme is designed to be a more streamlined process, but it still requires the issuance of a notice to the taxpayer. The Patna High Court's judgment emphasizes the importance of adhering to the procedural requirements, even in the context of a block assessment.

items.title: Wrong Mention of Section 69 Instead of 69A Does Not Invalidate Addition: ITAT Hyderabad

items.body:
Background & Legal Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, empowers the Assessing Officer (AO) to make additions to the total income of an assessee under various provisions, including Section 69 and Section 69A. Section 69 of the Act deals with unexplained investments, whereas Section 69A pertains to unexplained money, etc. The distinction between these two sections is crucial, as it determines the nature of the addition and the applicable provisions. The AO's decision to invoke a particular section must be based on a thorough analysis of the facts and circumstances of the case. In this context, the ITAT Hyderabad's recent judgment assumes significance, as it delves into the implications of wrongly mentioning Section 69 instead of Section 69A while making an addition.

The ITAT's decision is grounded in the principles of statutory interpretation, which emphasize the importance of adhering to the language and intent of the legislation. Section 253(1) of the Income Tax Act, which governs the powers of the ITAT, does not explicitly address the issue of incorrect section mentions. However, the Supreme Court's judgment in the case of CIT vs. Suresh N. Gupta (2003) provides guidance on this matter, highlighting the need for the AO to apply the correct provision based on the facts of the case. The ITAT's ruling can be seen as an extension of this principle, emphasizing the importance of precision in invoking the relevant sections of the Act.

The CBDT's circulars and notifications also provide valuable context to this issue. For instance, Circular No. 14/2019, dated December 31, 2019, deals with the procedure for making additions under Section 69 and Section 69A. Although this circular does not directly address the issue of incorrect section mentions, it underscores the importance of following the prescribed procedures and guidelines while making additions. The ITAT's decision, in this case, serves as a reminder to tax authorities and practitioners to exercise diligence in invoking the correct provisions of the Act.

Facts of the Case

The facts of the case reveal that the AO had discovered excess cash and stock shortages during the course of the assessment proceedings. Arguments & Contentions

The Revenue, on the other hand, argued that the incorrect mention of the section would not affect the validity of the addition. The Revenue contended that the AO had correctly applied the provisions of the Act, and that the incorrect mention of the section was a mere technicality. The Revenue relied on various circulars and notifications issued by the CBDT, including Circular No. 14/2019, to support its contention.

The ITAT considered the arguments and contentions of both parties, and examined the relevant provisions of the Act. The tribunal's decision turned on the interpretation of the language and intent of the relevant provisions, as well as the principles of statutory interpretation. The ITAT's judgment provides a detailed analysis of the relevant provisions, and serves as a valuable guide for tax authorities and practitioners.

Tribunal's Ruling & Reasoning

The ITAT Hyderabad upheld the addition made by the AO, despite the incorrect mention of Section 69 instead of Section 69A. The tribunal held that the incorrect mention of the section would not invalidate the addition, as the AO had correctly applied the provisions of the Act. The ITAT's decision was based on a detailed analysis of the relevant provisions, including Section 69 and Section 69A of the Income Tax Act.

The tribunal's ruling emphasizes the importance of adhering to the principles of statutory interpretation, while also highlighting the need for tax authorities to follow the correct procedures and guidelines while making additions.
